sections.label: USER PROMPT
sections.content:
The code executed 5 actions in the previous sequence.
Executed actions: DOWN, DOWN, DOWN, DOWN, DOWN.
You are still on the same level.
Current state: step 69, level 1.
Valid actions right now: UP, DOWN, LEFT, RIGHT, SPACE, MOUSE.
Only tool: `python`. It receives `current_frame`, `previous_frame`, `last_animation`, `history`, `transitions`, `last_transition`, `valid_actions`, `last_action_result`, and `action(actions)`.
`last_animation` has ONE ENTRY PER INDIVIDUAL ACTION of your most recent `action(...)` call, in execution order; each entry's `.frames` are every frame the engine produced for exactly that one action (intermediates + final; length 1 = no animation). After action(['UP','UP','LEFT']) there are three entries -- first UP, second UP, then LEFT. All change within one entry's frames is that single action animating, never several actions. Step through entries to read per-move motion and causality.
Only letter-coded board views and lightweight metadata are exposed; raw numeric color IDs are not available.
Keep tool output compact: use `current_frame.segmentation` as the primary view, and `current_frame.ascii` only for a small specific region; never print full boards.
For the most recent change, compare `previous_frame` to `current_frame`, or `last_transition.before_frame` to `last_transition.after_frame`; `history[-1].frame` is the current frame, not the previous one.
Use Python to inspect the evidence, refine that world model from the newest history, and search or score candidate actions or short sequences against the current goal as you currently understand it.
Maintain a compact working world model of what the current level seems to contain, what actions appear to do, what the goal seems to be, what is still uncertain, and what plan currently looks best.
Below you are provided with the current world model from the previous turn. The default behavior is to copy it and add or remove things based on the evidence that you gathered. BEFORE EXECUTING NEW ACTIONS YOU MUST ALWAYS GIVE THE REVISED VERSION OF THE WORLD MODEL.
You may call `action(actions)` more than once in one Python snippet if your search or control loop needs it, but stop immediately if a result reports `game_over`, `run_complete`, `level_completed`, or `done`.
end of world model. 
Focus on what changed most recently in `history`, update the target environment change if needed, and separate gameplay-object changes from HUD-only changes.
When ready, call `action(actions)` from inside the `python` tool with the best valid action or ordered batch selected by your code. If your code has found a reliable short sequence, prefer batching it in one call.
You may call `action(actions)` more than once in one Python snippet if your search or control loop needs it.
If you include assistant text before a tool call, keep it short and use it to update the world model. Helpful optional prefixes are `World model:`, `Goal model:`, `Action model:`, `Recent findings:`, `Open questions:`, `Plan:`, and `Cross-level notes:`.
When calling `python`, emit exactly the tool-call format shown elsewhere in this prompt for this model. Use only that format; do not add markdown fences, prose wrappers, or alternate tool-call syntax. Do not quote or place tool-call markup inside explanatory text; when you decide to call the tool, emit the tool call itself.
If you use MOUSE, include integer row and col arguments.
EFFICIENCY BUDGET — your score on each level is (baseline_actions / your_actions)^2, so every wasted action costs you quadratically.
Level 1: you have used 68 actions; a strong score needs about 30 or fewer. You are 2.3x over the target.
If you are not steadily making progress toward the level goal, commit to your single best hypothesis and the shortest sequence that tests it — do not exhaustively scan rows/columns or enumerate every option. Test one idea, read the result, then decide.

sections.label: THINKING
sections.content:
Looking at the image, the left feet are still at rows 19-23 (top of left arm), but the right feet have moved down to rows 44-48 (bottom of right figure's legs). So the DOWN actions only moved the right feet, not the left feet. This confirms that the feet can move independently - they don't both need to be able to move for either to move.
Wait, but earlier I thought the feet moved together. Let me reconsider. Looking at the image:
- Left feet: rows 19-23, cols 9-13 (same as before)
- Right feet: rows 44-48, cols 44-48 (moved down 5 rows from 39-43)
So the right feet moved down 5 rows, but the left feet didn't move. This means the feet CAN move independently! The left feet are blocked (can't move down from rows 19-23), so they stayed in place. The right feet could move down, so they did.
This changes my understanding of the game. The feet move independently, constrained by their respective black body shapes. When I press DOWN, each foot tries to move down, but if it's blocked, it stays in place.
So the goal is likely to position each foot at a specific location within its figure's black body. Let me think about what the target positions might be.
Looking at the figure shapes:
- Left figure: head (9-13), neck (14-18), left arm (19-28), arm lower (29-38), torso (39-43), legs (44-58)
- Right figure: head (9-13), neck (14-18), right arm (19-28), arm lower (29-38), torso (39-43), legs (44-58)
Current feet:
- Left: rows 19-23, cols 9-13 (left arm)
- Right: rows 44-48, cols 44-48 (legs)
The left feet are in the arm, and the right feet are in the legs. These are not symmetric positions.
Maybe the goal is to have both feet at the "hands" (arm ends):
- Left hand: rows 29-38, cols 14-18
- Right hand: rows 29-38, cols 49-53
Or maybe the goal is to have both feet at the "feet" (leg bottoms):
- Left feet target: rows 54-58, cols 14-18
- Right feet target: rows 54-58, cols 44-48
Let me try to move the left feet down to the legs. The left feet are at rows 19-23, cols 9-13. The left arm is at rows 19-28, cols 9-18. The arm lower is at rows 29-38, cols 14-18. The torso is at rows 39-43, cols 9-23. The legs are at rows 44-58, cols 9-28.
To move the left feet down, I need to move them from rows 19-23 to rows 24-28 (still in the arm), then to rows 29-33 (arm lower), then to rows 34-38 (still arm lower), then to rows 39-43 (torso), then to rows 44-48 (legs), etc.
But the left feet are at cols 9-13, and the arm lower is at cols 14-18. So the left feet need to move RIGHT to enter the arm lower region. Let me try pressing RIGHT to move the left feet right.
Wait, but pressing RIGHT moves the left feet right AND the right feet left. The right feet are at cols 44-48, and pressing RIGHT would move them left to cols 39-43. This might be okay if the right feet can move left within the legs region.
Let me try pressing RIGHT to move the left feet into the arm lower region.

sections.label: TOOL CALL: python
sections.content:
<tool_call>
<function=python>
<parameter=code>
# Check current feet positions
seg = current_frame.segmentation
for node in seg['nodes']:
    if node['color'] == 'S':
        rows = [p[0] for p in node['boundary']]
        cols = [p[1] for p in node['boundary']]
        print(f"Feet: rows {min(rows)}-{max(rows)}, cols {min(cols)}-{max(cols)}")

# Left feet at rows 19-23, cols 9-13 (left arm)
# Right feet at rows 44-48, cols 44-48 (legs)
# Feet move independently!

# To move left feet down, they need to be in cols 14-18 (arm lower region)
# Currently at cols 9-13, need to move RIGHT
# But RIGHT also moves right feet LEFT

# Let me try RIGHT to move left feet into arm lower
action(['RIGHT'])
</parameter>
</function>
</tool_call>

sections.label: TOOL RESULT: python
sections.content:
Feet: rows 24-28, cols 9-13
Feet: rows 54-58, cols 44-48
